resources.string: https://github.com/esc0rtd3w/dev.perms.test
PermsTest is an Android diagnostics and testing toolkit for packages, privileged shell commands, files, networks, memory tools, save-data editing, debugging, plugins, logs, and VR-compatible panels.
Detect Wireless debugging ports for Internal Shizuku pairing.
Shizuku Sample
Memory Editor
Hex Editor
Disassembly
Special Tools
PermsTest
Starting PermsTest, restoring saved choices, and checking active tools
Getting the workspace ready
Choose Shizuku, Internal Shizuku, LADB, or System before privileged actions.
Turn on Debug Output before reproducing a problem so the app records useful status lines.
Use VR-compatible panels when normal overlay windows are not suitable for the headset.
Start FTP, HTTP, or SSH only while remote access must remain active.
Pick the target package/process first so scans, patches, and payloads stay scoped correctly.
Enable only the Web Interface sections you want reachable from a browser.
Use Package tools to launch, inspect, extract, install, uninstall, or change app state.
Switch Files to Shizuku access when protected paths or faster privileged copies are needed.
Run shell commands through the selected backend and check the exit code before trusting output.
Use split install options to choose ABI, language, and density entries from APKS/APKM/XAPK archives.
Check original bytes, patched bytes, masks, and package scope before applying payloads.
Open Hex Editor from a result row when nearby bytes need inspection before a write.
Use Disassembly for instruction context, branch targets, and Hex sync around an address.
Use Save Data presets with backups before writing modified save files.
Use Copy File To Root or Copy Folder To Root to publish content through HTTP.
Enable sleep mode only when FTP or HTTP must survive the screen turning off.
Start Multiplayer Link host before a guest device fetches shared status.
Mark a log session before testing so exported logs have a clear starting point.
Home Mode opens Android default-launcher selection after the launcher alias is enabled.
Settings apply missing defaults without overwriting choices you already saved.
Memory web controls use the same backend path as the native Memory tools.
Use Debugging tools to decompile, search, edit, rebuild, sign, and inspect APK changes.
Funny Animation Tooltips
Hide Main Page Banner
Dark (Default)
Custom Color
Gradient
Color Navigation Tabs
Automatically Apply Device Defaults
Enable VR Mode
Device Profile: Detecting profile, device, OS, CPU, DPI, language, and serial status
Preparing package lists and install actions
Preparing scanner, payload, Hex, and Disassembly tools
Preparing file browser shortcuts and actions
Checking FTP, HTTP, SSH, Web, and network tools
Preparing shell commands and backend output
Loading APK, smali, MITM, and report tools
Applying saved settings without changing user choices
Preparing web controls
Checking selected execution backend
Preparing installer options
Registering file handlers
Preparing save-data configs, presets, and backups
Preparing script editor
Opening log buffers
Applying theme colors
Preparing link controls
Checking popout panel settings
Refreshing service state
Checking update controls
Indexing payload editor state
Preparing smali job service
Resolving debugging paths
Checking startup cleanup
Checking install sources
Checking HTTP service hooks
Preparing FTP client views
Preparing file icons
Refreshing active notifications
Preparing package permission controls
Preparing payload apply guards
Checking Memory, Hex, and Disassembly sync
Loading Web Interface access settings
Preparing output panels
Use Unknown/Snapshot scan when the first value is not known.
Use Next Scan to reduce result count after the target value changes.
Patch checked rows only after confirming the address, type, and value.
Extract installed APKs to shared storage before rebuilding or inspecting them.
Create Debuggable Packages when an APK needs debug flags or user CA trust changes.
Search smali output first, then open only the file that needs editing.
Use FTP Client split view to transfer between local storage and a remote server.
Edit index.html when the HTTP root needs a custom landing page.
Use Mark Session before testing so exported logs have a clear starting point.
Use Archive Logs before clearing logs so the full PermsTest log folder is preserved in a timestamped ZIP.
VR panel mode keeps Memory, Hex, Disassembly, and Special Tools in separate tasks.
Open APK and split archives with the installer; use normal browsing for other files.
Active Memory, FTP, and HTTP service notifications are refreshed while their services remain active.
Refresh Shizuku status after restarting the server or changing Wireless Debugging state.
Package dropdowns can include debuggable packages even when running-only filtering is off.
Long-press Files entries for install, open-with, copy, move, rename, delete, path, and properties actions.
Enable only the Web Interface sections that should be reachable from a browser.
Freeze values only after confirming the target process and selected result rows.
Open branch or reference targets in Hex when disassembly shows an address worth checking.
Scripts can be edited in-app and staged from the shared scripts folder.
Keep backups and export reports before testing MITM or manifest patches.
Save Data Editor creates backups before preset writes when backup support is enabled.
Enable HTTP or FTP sleep mode only for sessions that need screen-off access.
Use /data/local/tmp staging when split installs need a shell-readable path.
Output height and truncation settings help keep long command results readable.
Custom theme colors affect the startup screen, app background, and optional navigation tabs.
VR App Tray diagnostics stay behind Debug Output so normal navigation remains quiet.
Use Check For Updates from About to download the latest PermsTest APK before installing it.
Custom update URLs can point to a GitHub release page, a latest-release endpoint, or a direct APK.
Downloaded update APKs are staged in a managed folder and cleaned by startup cache cleanup.
Open Any lets the Smali Editor load a .smali file outside the active disassembly folder.
Smali search runs through a foreground job so long searches can continue after navigation.
Debugging work paths use package names when a browsed APK matches an installed package.
External .smali files can open through the internal editor instead of the package installer path.
Payload dropdown colors show enabled and disabled payload state before opening JSON details.
Preserve masked bytes keeps wildcard regions from being overwritten during payload writes.
Tap an address in Details to open related tools without losing the current log position.
Tap a disassembly address to open Hex directly, or long-press for the context menu.
Tap Refresh again if a Memory package-list scan fails.
Startup cleanup removes managed scratch, import, debugging input, and update-download folders.
The About version row keeps update checks close to the installed version.
After an update APK downloads, Android package installer still performs the final install confirmation.
Reticulating APK splits
Polishing socket handles
Negotiating overlay gravity
Sorting density buckets
Buckling payload seatbelts
Warming up Shizuku
Feeding the log buffer
Aligning VR panels
Measuring byte soup
Teaching sockets manners
Letting payloads stretch
Checking APK shoelaces
Asking logs nicely
Convincing panels to behave
Dusting shell commands
Untangling split spaghetti
Installing tiny traffic cones
Rehearsing startup transitions
Consulting update gnome
Hanging smali hammocks
Finding cache broom
Sorting payload crayons
Lighting hex lanterns
Scheduling API pigeon
Giving permissions a pep talk
Timing debug snails
Counting HTTP cookies
Packing FTP backpack
Leaning update ladder
Folding smali map
Popping log bubbles
Teaching log archives to zip politely
Applying overlay tape
Handing installer helmet
Reticulating APK splits; ABI, language, and DPI entries are staying in their lanes.
Warming up Shizuku; privileged actions work better after permission is granted.
Sorting bytes by attitude; Hex Editor will still show the real order.
Feeding the log buffer; Debug Output adds the useful breadcrumbs.
Negotiating overlay gravity; VR panels get separate handling when enabled.
Packing hex snacks; check original bytes before writing patched bytes.
Briefing the HTTP doorman; only enabled Web Interface sections get in.
Digging an FTP tunnel; background mode keeps transfers reachable when enabled.
Opening VR windows carefully; panel mode avoids raw overlay trouble.
Checking payload seatbelts; masks and package scope help prevent wrong writes.
Zipping APK parts without pinching the manifest.
Untangling smali threads; search before editing keeps the patch small.
Finding matching memory socks; Next Scan removes the lonely ones.
Trying on launcher hats; Home Mode still opens Android default selection.
Calibrating the safety goggles; backups and logs make testing recoverable.
Measuring byte soup; the Hex Editor promises to label every spoonful.
Teaching sockets manners; FTP and HTTP still wait for explicit start buttons.
Letting payloads stretch; masks keep the wrong bytes out of yoga class.
Checking APK shoelaces; split archives should not trip over ABI folders.
Asking logs nicely; Debug Output makes them answer with more details.
Convincing panels to behave; VR panels stay behind the VR mode gate.
Dusting shell commands; bundled tools get staged before heavy lifting.
Untangling split spaghetti; density, language, and ABI strands stay labeled.
Installing tiny traffic cones; install staging should know which lane it is using.
Rehearsing startup transitions; the final loading text keeps the main screen reveal smooth.
Baking permission cookies; grants still depend on backend authority.
Packing network snacks; sleep mode should only camp out when needed.
Picking memory lint; Next Scan keeps only the pieces that changed correctly.
Building an HTTP sandwich; index.html gets the top slice.
Lining up debug ducks; reports help prove which patch waddled where.
Consulting the update gnome; it prefers release pages with APKs attached.
Hanging smali hammocks; long searches can rest in the foreground service.
Finding the cache broom; managed temp folders should leave before breakfast.
Sorting payload crayons; green means enabled and red means sit this one out.
Lighting hex lanterns; selected addresses are easier to find with fewer shadows.
Scheduling the API pigeon; custom update servers still need a usable APK trail.
Giving permissions a pep talk; Android still makes the final call.
Timing debug snails; background smali jobs keep crawling after the tab changes.
Counting HTTP cookies; Web Interface gates decide who gets dessert.
Packing the FTP backpack; remote paths travel better when the root is readable.
Leaning the update ladder carefully; package installer does the climbing.
Folding the smali map; package-name paths keep the same trail from APK or app.
Popping log bubbles; noisy refresh messages should not flood Details.
Teaching log archives to zip politely; Clear Logs keeps archived reports out of the broom pile.
Handing installer helmet; split archives can still bump into ABI corners.
Shizuku API
Allows access to Shizuku privileged API.
Colorize App Dropdown

Overview
• PermsTest is a toolbox for Android package, permission, file, network, logging, debugging, plugin, and memory tasks.
• Start on Main > Backend and choose the access method that fits the task: Shizuku, Internal Shizuku, LADB, root, or System.
• Buttons remain disabled until the required file, package, backend, setting, or editor content is ready.
• Check the bottom output pane after each action. It shows status, paths, command output, exit codes, and useful errors.
• For troubleshooting, enable Debug Output, mark a log session, reproduce the issue once, then use Full Diagnostic or Archive Logs.

Main / Startup
• Main > Backend controls the access mode used by privileged actions. Tap Refresh after starting or reconnecting a backend.
• Device defaults can apply recommended settings for the current device without overwriting later manual choices.
• Home contains launcher shortcuts, package actions, popout panel entry points, and status rows for common services.
• Popout panels mirror supported sections so you can keep tools visible while working in another tab.

Backends
• Shizuku uses the installed Shizuku service after Android grants PermsTest permission.
• Internal Shizuku uses Wireless Debugging pairing/start controls and must show a ready binder before privileged actions.
• LADB uses Android Wireless Debugging and may need reconnecting when Android changes the port.
• Root/Magisk actions require su access. System mode stays inside normal app permissions.
• Backend failures usually mean the service is stopped, permission was denied, wireless debugging changed, or storage/security policy blocked the command.

Shell Tab
• Run commands through the selected backend and review stdout, stderr, and exit code before trusting the result.
• Quick Commands provide common Android diagnostics and package/file commands.
• Custom Commands can be saved, grouped, reordered, imported, exported, filled, or run.
• Scanned Device Binaries and Bundled Bins help locate tools. Review write, delete, and reset commands before running them.

Packages Tab
• APK Installer accepts APK, APKS, APKM, and XAPK files. Select the source, review split choices, then tap Install.
• Android still enforces signatures, version codes, ABI compatibility, target-SDK policy, and install-source rules.
• Allow Downgrade does not bypass signature mismatch. Use replace/uninstall flows only when you understand the app-data impact.
• Permissions & State inspects package permissions, grants/revokes supported permissions, and enables/disables packages after review.
• Package State List saves and loads All, Enabled, or Disabled snapshots. Loaded rows start unchecked and color-code saved state.
• Debloat presets are review lists. They do not disable anything until you confirm Disable Packages.

Files Tab
• Use normal Android file access for shared files or Shizuku/root access for protected paths supported by the selected backend.
• Long-press files or folders for open, install, copy, cut, paste, rename, delete, path, and properties actions.
• Split-pane mode transfers between two locations. Confirm the active pane and destination before copying or moving.
• Root directory size scans are optional because they can be slow or blocked by device policy.

APK Editor
• APK Editor can inspect manifests, decode/build with the bundled apktool-compatible helper, rename supported labels, sign outputs, and install test builds.
• Manifest-only edits do not require smali repack. Use smali repack for actual smali changes.
• Resource-backed labels and complex APK resources may require manual review after rebuild.
• Keep original APKs and app data backed up before editing or installing modified packages.

Debugging Tab
• Shared APK/DEX source controls feed Smali Output, Java Output, DEX selection, and class listing actions.
• Smali Output disassembles APK/DEX content, searches the workspace, edits smali, and assembles/rebuilds outputs.
• Java Output runs jadx-go to generate read-only Java views for navigation.
• DEX to Java can process all top-level DEX entries or only the selected DEX entry, and can zip output when complete.
• Generated Java is for reading and navigation. Edit smali when you need rebuildable output.
• Large Java and smali files use the shared virtual editor path to avoid UI stalls.

Memory Tab
• Select the target package and process before attach, scan, patch, dump, or opening memory tools.
• Use Exact when the first value is known or Unknown/Snapshot when it is not. Use Next Scan after values change.
• Confirm addresses, value types, masks, and package scope before writing memory.
• Memory Hex Editor edits the selected live process. File Hex Editor edits regular files.
• Keep memory writes small, verified, and reversible whenever possible.

Memory Payloads
• Payloads store package-scoped original bytes, patched bytes, masks, and optional section markers.
• Find/Load helps locate matching bytes when addresses change between runs.
• Apply All and Apply On Attach should be tested one payload at a time on unfamiliar targets.
• Payload shortcuts launch the target app and apply the selected payload behavior according to the shortcut settings.

VR / Popout Panels
• Normal phones and tablets use the standard UI path.
• Enable VR Mode and VR-compatible panel options only on headsets that need Activity-hosted panels.
• VR-specific UI behavior is gated by VR Mode and should not affect normal phone/tablet behavior while VR Mode is off.
• Popout panels share the same feature state as the main tabs.

Network Tab
• FTP Server, HTTP Server, SSH Server, and Web Interface expose files or controls over the network. Use trusted networks only.
• SSH Client connects to trusted hosts and runs commands from the Network tab.
• Enable sleep operation only while a server must stay available with the screen off.
• FTP Client transfers between local and remote paths. Confirm selected local/remote rows before upload, download, delete, or rename.
• Host Tests, HTTP Request, and TCP Port Check provide quick connectivity diagnostics.

SSH Server
• SSH Server provides password-protected local-network SSH/SFTP access from a foreground service with an ongoing notification.
• SFTP uses the configured root folder. Shell access is disabled by default and starts a normal app-owned shell, not root.
• Keep the password private, reset the host key only when you expect SSH clients to warn, and do not expose SSH on an untrusted network.

HTTP Server / Web Interface
• HTTP Server serves the selected root/index and is stopped from inside the app.
• Web Interface sections are controlled by app settings. Disabled sections should be hidden and rejected by API calls.
• Web Interface FTP controls can start FTP only when it is stopped and stop FTP only when it is running.
• Do not expose package, file, memory, or shell controls on an untrusted network.

Scripts Tab
• Create, edit, save, refresh, and run shell scripts from /sdcard/dev.perms.test/scripts.
• Scripts run through the selected backend, so backend readiness and command review matter.
• Script-capable plugins use the same explicit-tap backend path and policy checks.
• Keep destructive commands separated from read-only diagnostics.

Tools Tab
• Intent Launcher sends configured Android intents. Intent Receiver captures intents sent to declared receiver aliases.
• Permission Tester creates launchable, manifest-only, or selected-app test APKs with chosen permissions.
• Activity Manager lists exported/non-exported activities and can launch or create shortcuts where Android allows it.
• Save Data Editor edits supported save data using package-specific profiles and creates backups before supported writes.
• Calculator, ASCII/Hex Converter, Alarms/Timers, text editor, file hex editor, and helper tools remain available from Tools.

Plugins Tab
• Import Plugin stages a .ptp or .zip package. Enable the plugin, then tap one of its action buttons.
• Bundled plugin choices start unchecked. Restore Selected reinstalls checked bundled plugins; Clean Invalid removes unparseable staged folders.
• Plugin Editor can create, import, validate, save, package, and export plugin.json/ui.json-based plugins.
• Declarative plugins use supported PermsTest actions. Script plugins require explicit review before running. Trusted-Dex plugins require matching trusted payload details.
• Disabled plugins remain inert. Plugins do not launch APK components, services, receivers, boot handlers, or hidden background work.

Logging Tab
• Filter and Lines control Logcat captures. Save To File stores the next raw Logcat capture instead of dumping it into the output pane.
• Save Output stores the visible bottom output pane. Share sends the most recently saved/exported file.
• Full Diagnostic saves output, exports lifetime log, captures Logcat All, and runs the diagnostic/debug capture group in one action.
• Enable Lifetime Log keeps structured actions across sessions. Turning it off disables View Lifetime, Export Lifetime, Clear Lifetime, and Mark Session.
• Archive Logs creates a timestamped ZIP of public logs, lifetime logs, plugin reports, app-private snapshots, and diagnostic captures.
• Clear Logs removes active logs but keeps existing archives.

Settings Tab
• Automatically Apply Device Defaults applies missing profile defaults while keeping later manual choices.
• Enable Popout Panels controls supported groupbox windows. Enable VR Mode controls headset-specific UI behavior.
• Colorize App Dropdown changes app-list status coloring. Fat Dropdown Scrollbars makes dropdown scroll thumbs easier to use.
• Enable File Open Handler lets other apps send supported files to PermsTest and should stay on unless you choose otherwise.
• Debug Output adds more status lines for troubleshooting.

Storage Layout
• Shared root: /sdcard/dev.perms.test
• Logs and archives: logs and log_archives
• Plugins: plugins and plugin_exports
• Debug APKs and workspaces: debug_packages and debugging
• Java/smali output: debugging/java and debugging/smali
• Scripts, payloads, dumps, package states, and save configs use their matching feature folders.

API / Integration Guides
• API Guide buttons explain shared controls, Android integration, execution backends, APK/debugging tools, and the Plugin API.
• Plugin API documentation lists supported plugin.json fields, UI editing, action types, host APIs, native handlers, bundled examples, and Plugin Editor steps.
• Guides describe available behavior; they do not grant Android permissions, bypass signatures, or bypass platform security.

Included Components
• Shizuku, LADB, smali/baksmali, apktool-go, jadx-go, medit, FTP, HTTP, SSH, and Android PackageInstaller each have their own limits and logs.
• APK Editor uses apktool-go for APK decode/build tasks, then captures command output and handles signing/install steps in the app.
• Java Output uses jadx-go for DEX/APK Java-view generation and keeps generated Java read-only for navigation.
• Logs under logs/apktool and logs/jadx-go show command lines, output paths, exit codes, timing, and summary counters.

Safe Use
• Android version, OEM restrictions, SELinux, storage access, backend readiness, target-app protection, and app signing affect available actions.
• Confirm the target package/path before install, uninstall, clear-data, delete, patch, memory-write, save-data-write, or remote-access actions.
• For a useful report, enable Debug Output, mark a session, reproduce once, then use Full Diagnostic or Archive Logs.
• Prefer small test changes, keep backups, and verify output before applying bulk package, file, memory, or install operations.

Shizuku
https://github.com/RikkaApps/Shizuku

Shizuku API
https://github.com/RikkaApps/Shizuku-API

LADB
https://github.com/tytydraco/LADB

apk-medit
https://github.com/sterrasec/apk-medit

Android-Disassembler
https://github.com/yhs0602/Android-Disassembler

FTPClient for Android
https://codeberg.org/qwerty287/ftpclient

Apache Commons Net
https://commons.apache.org/proper/commons-net/

smali / baksmali
https://github.com/JesusFreke/smali

Apktool source
https://github.com/iBotPeaches/Apktool

JADX source
https://github.com/skylot/jadx

Android PackageInstaller
https://developer.android.com/reference/android/content/pm/PackageInstaller

Android Storage Access Framework
https://developer.android.com/guide/topics/providers/document-provider
Scanned Device Binaries
Manage Bundled Bins
adb
reboot
gzip
gunzip
setenforce …
screenrecord
start
sysctl
fastboot
tcpdump
getevent
lsof
Confirm setenforce

Run: %1$s
This attempts to change SELinux enforcement state.
Run
Cancel
base64
clear
free
vmstat
watch
curl
ip
ping
sha256sum
xxd
yes
zcat
zipinfo
ziptool
dos2unix
tty
umount
unix2dos
Preparing release selector
Checking update channel settings
Preparing silent update install path
Reading release assets
Filtering APK assets
Preparing managed update staging
Checking installer backend
Preparing diagnostic status
Indexing smali workspace
Checking payload state
Preparing web status bridge
Checking file access
Applying VR-compatible settings
Preparing network keep-alive settings
Preparing log tail buffers
Allow Downgrade opens a selectable release list for the chosen update channel instead of auto-picking one APK.
Include Pre-Releases checks the GitHub prerelease channel before stable releases.
Downgrade installs still use Android package rules, so package signatures must match the installed app.
Auto Update uses the selected Release or Pre-Release channel and keeps manual Check For Updates unchanged.
Silent updates use the selected privileged backend and fall back to logging if installation cannot complete.
Downloaded update APKs are staged in managed app storage and cleaned by startup cache cleanup.
Open Any in the smali editor allows external smali files without requiring a current disassembly folder.
Smali searches run through the foreground job service so long searches can continue after tab navigation.
Payload masks preserve wildcard bytes so runtime-specific pointer bytes can remain untouched.
Disassembly address taps can synchronize the Hex Editor to the selected instruction target.
Web Interface access settings hide disabled sections and reject requests to them.
FTP and HTTP background modes use foreground services so Android is less likely to stop active sessions.
Files tab Shizuku copy paths help operations work across storage locations that normal app access cannot reach.
VR panel mode keeps headset window behavior gated separately from normal tablet and phone overlays.
Lifetime logs make it easier to compare startup, install, network, and memory behavior across test runs.
Consulting release oracle
Sorting pre-release gremlins
Tying update shoes
Dusting asset shelf
Pouring backend tea
Folding smali laundry
Checking payload weather
Measuring VR tape
Unclogging log fountain
Pairing network socks
Knitting hex mittens
Lighting permission lantern
Butterizing file biscuit
Organizing shell parade
Washing cache sweater
Consulting release oracle; it demands one APK and a respectful version tag.
Sorting pre-release gremlins; the stable ones keep wearing tiny lab coats.
Tying update shoes so the installer does not trip over its own package name.
Dusting asset shelf; one APK rolled behind the changelog again.
Pouring backend tea; Shizuku likes it privileged but not too hot.
Folding smali laundry; every method gets its own tiny drawer.
Checking payload weather; cloudy with a chance of preserved wildcard bytes.
Measuring VR tape twice so headset windows do not become postage stamps.
Unclogging log fountain; one debug line tried to become a waterfall.
Pairing network socks; FTP found one, HTTP found the other.
Knitting hex mittens so cold bytes stop pretending to be strings.
Lighting permission lantern; Android storage caves are dramatic today.
Butterizing file biscuit; crunchy paths taste better with Shizuku.
Organizing shell parade; every command must march in quoting order.
Washing cache sweater; lint removed from three temp folders and a suspicious APK pocket.
Preparing memory package running labels
Reading Android OS badge
Preparing package dropdown fast path
Checking update picker paths
Preparing smali intent routing
Preparing install debug logs
Applying memory dropdown modes
Loading About and API guides
Checking privileged shell routing
Checking payload mask settings
Preparing FileProvider URI sharing
Refreshing network service status
Sizing VR-safe buttons
Preparing package extractors
Initializing missing settings defaults
Show Running Packages labels active processes; Only Show Running Packages filters the target list.
The Main tab device info row includes Android OS version beside profile, device, CPU, DPI, language, and serial.
Package dropdowns use cached installed-app data where possible so selection panels open faster.
Allow Downgrade uses a selectable release list while normal update checks keep using the newest candidate.
External smali files can route into the internal editor without being mistaken for package installs.
Package install debug logs use the same result format no matter where the install was started.
Show Running Packages labels active processes, while Only Show Running Packages filters the Memory target list.
The About page summarizes real app workflows and keeps links/update controls easy to find.
Shizuku, Internal Shizuku, and LADB stay separated so backend errors are easier to read.
Payload write verification compares expected bytes after preserving wildcard mask regions.
Managed FileProvider URI grants keep downloaded updates and staged files readable by Android installers.
FTP, HTTP, and Web Interface controls show service state without changing roots unexpectedly.
VR-compatible panels use slightly larger controls where clipped text is likely inside headset windows.
New settings defaults are filled only when missing, so saved preferences survive updates.
Extract installed packages from Packages, Debugging, or debug-package tools when a local APK copy is needed.
Teaching dropdowns to sprint
Trying on ptrace hat
Fishing for release fish
Sweeping smali confetti
Stretching VR buttons
Toasting shell marshmallows
Counting hex spoons
Blowing payload bubbles
Feeding log hamster
Opening file umbrella
Flipping network pancakes
Sorting package socks
Watering settings garden
Loading update wagon
Guarding cache sandcastle
Teaching dropdowns to sprint; one package list requested tiny running shoes and a dramatic countdown.
Trying on ptrace hat; Android 9 says it fits, Android 10 says fashion rules changed.
Fishing for release fish; prereleases keep wearing shiny beta scales.
Sweeping smali confetti; every label jumped out of a method and yelled surprise.
Stretching VR buttons so headset pixels stop chewing the update text.
Toasting shell marshmallows; quotes go on the stick before the command catches fire.
Counting hex spoons; byte 0x2A insists it is the fancy one.
Blowing payload bubbles; masked bytes float away but politely come back after write verify.
Feeding log hamster; it runs faster when Debug Output gives it timestamps.
Opening file umbrella; scoped storage rain is in the forecast again.
Flipping network pancakes; FTP wants syrup, HTTP wants headers.
Sorting package socks; debuggable ones are blue and somehow still missing one sock.
Watering settings garden; defaults grow only where preferences are empty.
Loading update wagon; direct APK links get front-row seats.
Guarding cache sandcastle; startup cleanup brought a very official little shovel.
Randomize Current Tooltips
Animation Category
Startup Animation
Randomize All Tooltips
Preparing high-level diagnostics
Use this category when the startup screen needs relaxed-but-accurate diagnostics while privileged services settle.
Rolling perfect 0x420 vibes
Reticulating snack clouds; the byte garden insists it can smell colors now.
Aligning starship-grade package scanners
Release, prerelease, and direct APK paths are checked before the jump to install space.
Convincing tiny droids to sort APK assets
A very dramatic protocol droid translated chmod into beeps and complained about sand.
Checking Android runtime gates
Device profile, OS version, backend mode, and privileged capability checks are staged before the UI opens.
Giving the framework a sensible hat
Android politely asked if every service could stop standing in the doorway at once.
Preparing memory target tools
Select a package/process, then use running-state filters, payload masks, Hex sync, and attach controls as needed.
Teaching memory addresses to wear nametags
One pointer hid behind a couch; the scanner found it because it was glowing suspiciously.
Heating smali workspace paths
Use Disassemble, Open Any, Search, and Rebuild for the selected smali/APK workspace.
Sharpening tiny smali chisels
The labels formed a union and demanded better line numbers and a coffee machine.
Binding network service controls
FTP, HTTP, client browsing, background mode, and access-gate status lines are prepared.
Polishing router goblins
HTTP brought headers, FTP brought snacks, and neither remembered the napkins.
Preparing file access
Choose standard or Shizuku file access, then use row menus for open, copy, move, install, and properties.
Teaching folders to use indoor voices
A ZIP file tried to unzip itself for attention and immediately regretted the spotlight.
Checking update channel settings
Release, prerelease, downgrade picker, custom URL, and direct APK handling are staged cleanly.
Training update pigeons
One prerelease wore a fake mustache and still got sorted by version number.
Preparing VR-safe panels
Enable VR mode only for headset-friendly panels and controls; normal tablet behavior remains separate.
Measuring headset pixels with a spoon
The VR panel asked for more elbow room and then floated five feet to the left.
Staging debug package workflows
Use Debugging for debuggable APKs, MITM reports, smali jobs, install logs, and package workspaces.
Inflating the debug circus tent
The stack trace juggler dropped three exceptions and called it experimental choreography.
Preparing shell command runners
Choose Shizuku or LADB, run the command, then check captured output and command status.
Lighting torches in shell dungeon
The prompt rolled initiative; stdout crit, stderr missed, exit code looked concerned.
Opening save-data editing vault
Select a package/config, create a backup, then review section-scoped edits before applying them.
Dusting tiny save-data vaults
A config key wore a monocle and insisted it was schema-compliant royalty.
Sorting APK workshop tools
Review split choices, staging mode, rebuild options, and extraction paths before installing.
Tying APK shoelaces
A base APK and three splits asked who brought the ABI chart.
Preparing permission desk controls
Select a package and permission/state action before using Grant, Revoke, Enable, or Disable.
Stamping permission paperwork
The permission clerk approved READ_SNACKS but denied WRITE_CHAOS pending review.
Opening operational logbook
Use Logcat, Save Output, Mark Session, Lifetime Log, and Archive Logs to capture the information you need.
Feeding the logbook hamster
It runs faster when timestamps arrive, but gets dramatic around stack traces.
Starting web interface bridge checks
Set the HTTP root/index and Web Interface access sections before starting remote controls.
Teaching the web bridge manners
The browser knocked twice, brought cookies, and asked if localhost was home.
Preparing payload lab benches
Payload JSON, masks, preserve bytes, auto-apply, and verification output are staged carefully.
Putting goggles on payloads
Masked bytes promised to stay invisible and then immediately wore neon shoes.
Aligning hex grid tools
Hex rows, ASCII sync, selection nudges, read/write size, and address jumps are prepared.
Counting hex spoons again
Byte 0xFF demanded a cape; byte 0x00 requested a quiet corner.
Checking controller room signals
Use input diagnostics and device defaults when testing controller-style controls.
Asking buttons to form a line
The controller reported every button was player one and requested a tiny trophy.
Warming garage-lab diagnostics
Use the available checks, logs, and update tools to diagnose the current device state.
Calibrating the garage raccoon
It tightened two bolts, labeled a cable, and stole exactly one 10mm socket.
